responseSet.title: From Disclosure to Foreclosure: Reply to Commentary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Disclosure to Foreclosure: Reply to Commentary

Samuel Gerson PHD

Samuel Gerson

2811 Regent Street
Berkeley, CA 94705

Samuel Gerson  

How shall we align our theories and techniques of psychoanalysis with contemporary understandings of our inevitable participation in the reproduction of social and economic relations that privilege the dominant? This challenge has hovered about, stimulating and troubling our profession from its inception. Yet, in spite of the commitments of many, we remain without a method that incorporates an appreciation of the ubiquitous nature of the dynamics of privilege and prejudice with our understandings, via explorations of the transference and countertransfrence, of how our patients' dilemmas are unconsciously anchored in their own unique histories and systems of meaning. The emergence of two-person perspectives on the psychoanalytic process, with their emphasis on analysts' participation in the creation of process and meaning, offers new possibilities for engaging these issues. Few psychoanalytic authors, however, have specifically addressed the influence of cultural affiliations and identities on the analyst's subjectivity, clinical process, and technique (see Leary, 1997, for a notable exception).

It is in this context that Layton and Bertone attempt to impress upon us that thinking about analysts' subjectivity must extend beyond the personal and interpersonal dynamics of the moment and give expression to an awareness of our place in the hierarchies of gender, sexuality, race, and class that inform all subjectivity; to do otherwise is to become complicit in replicating oppressive social forms. To my mind, this is an enlightened agenda—one to which an intersubjectively oriented psychoanalysis is uniquely suited and to which we can best contribute by supporting collegial communication of our efforts with patients.

Samuel Gerson, Ph.D. is Faculty, Supervising Analyst, and President of the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; and Associate Professor, California School of Professional Psychology.

Reply to Commentaries

Paul Williams PHD

Paul Williams

1/15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW35UJ
England
e-mail: 100576.1235@compuserve.com

Paul Williams  

Embarras de richesses best describes my reaction to the commentaries. It is unusual to have one's work commented on in such depth and I am grateful to the contributors from whom I have learnt a great deal. I shall try to reply to each and create some links with my paper. Doing so will involve commenting briefly on my view of the psychodynamics and origins of psychotic thinking. There are, I think, differences in perspective (or psychoanalytic vertices, to borrow Bion's, 1967, term) that have emerged here and that it would be helpful to debate. I am grateful for the opportunity to do so as the more the problems of severe disturbance can be discussed, the better it is likely to be for psychoanalysts, who are increasingly viewed as being at risk of losing their role in the provision of effective treatment for severe and enduring mental illnesses.

Margaret Black

Margaret Black's discussion is, as I see it, is most representative of the intersubjectivist tradition exemplified by Psychoanalytic Dialogues and offers the most significant differences in theoretical emphasis between us. There are, as Black points out, many areas of agreement, and I shall not dwell on these except to thank Black for her sensitive support for this kind of difficult work. The most important problem areas for me in her discussion concern (a) her emphasis on relation imagery in the organization of personality, (b) the primarily subjective reality constructs employed by the patient and the growth of dissociative structures, and (c) the need for a compliant but active environment to facilitate the redevelopment of self and ego. These are contrasted with my emphasis on unconscious ideation, “linear” mental development as Black sees it and my apparent need to “lock out” Miss A's disturbance by consigning it to the category of a psychotic self.

[[RunningHead]]

Fracturing Drive or Fracturing Connections—Relational Considerations: Commentary on Paper by Paul Williams

Margaret Black C.S.W.

Margaret Black

251 West 71st Street
New York, NY 10023

Margaret Black  

Intensive work with severely disturbed patients is not easy. Paul Williams warns us of this early in his report. “Any post hoc coherence this paper might convey should not minimize the perplexities and confusions that suffused the analysis.” Unrelenting emotional strain, affective assaults, and feelings of disorientation and deadness are only some of the experiences discouraging analysts from making long-term commitments to this kind of clinical work. Within the group of clinicians who have survived these challenges, Wilfred Bion has been a highly influential presence and with good reason. Placing disturbing mental content in some separate and identifiable place—imagery evoked by Bion's useful concepts of projective identification and containment—is deeply resonant with the subjective experiences of patient and analyst embarking on these challenging journeys. Patients feel “inhabitated”; analysts can come to value the ward key as guaranteeing a reassuring ritual of “locking in” the insanity at the end of the day.

Williams's work with Miss A reflects a personal amalgam of theoretical and clinical contributions drawn from the Kleinian tradition and enriched by currents from the British Middle School. My own thinking, also a personal amalgam, draws as well on British Middle School contributions, but incorporates, in addition, facets of the American traditions of self psychology, intersubjectivity theory and contemporary relational/interpersonal psychoanalysis.

Margaret Black, C.S.W., is Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute, Postgraduate Center for Mental Health, New York City, and Board Director and Director of Continuing Education, National Institute for the Psychotherapies. New York City.

© 1998 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentary

James Hansell PHD

James Hansell

3251 Bluett Road
Ann Arbor, MI 48105
jhansell@umich.edu

James Hansell  

Judith Butler's astute commentary offers more of the elegant insights that are characteristic of her work and that inspired me to write my piece initially. It is a privelege to reply.

In her comments, I take Butler to be raising crucial questions not just about my essay, but about the entire project of mapping the landscape of gender identity. In two distinct ways, she focuses on the fundamental question: What are the limiting parameters of any effort to chart this territory? First, Butler asks us to consider whether the relativity of every particular cultural and historical context fundamentally compromises any effort to firmly locate gender identity and its developmental history. Second, she wonders whether the very arguments that she and I use to deconstruct gender as a natural category call into question any attempt to formulate developmental accounts of gender identity, such as those I propose in my essay.

To the first of these two questions, I find a relatively straightforward answer. Naturally, any account of gender identity relies on shared, particularistic cultural presuppositions and is context-dependent. Does this make it impossible to universalize? Of course. Does it mean such accounts are useless? Of course not. They are useful when one is mindful of their contextual limits, just as it is very useful to speak Russian in Russia but not very useful anywhere else.

I find Butler's second question more complex. My response leads directly to the heart of the issues that I hoped to raise in my essay, and that might be illustrated best with a clinical example. Consider the following commonplace psychoanalytic situation. A male heterosexual patient embarks on an uncharacteristic period of sexual promiscuity and frantically pursues stereotypically masculine activities as his male analyst's summer vacation approaches. When asked about this change in his behavior, the patient at first says only, “What's to explain? I'm a guy!”—perfectly illustrating Butler's thesis regarding the defensive, retroactive use of gender as a natural, explanatory category.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychosis and the Other: Commentary on Paper by Paul Williams

John Muller PHD

John Muller

Stockbridge, MA 01262

John Muller  

Admiration and caution should mark the stance of a discussant upon reading an analytic treatment of such a disturbed patient, leading to evident positive results, and articulated in such a clear and moving manner. The admiration is both for the patient, for showing great courage in the face of ongoing psychotic liabilities, and for the analyst, for keeping his wits about him and for showing such respect for the patient's courage that he took it upon himself to articulate the exemplary aspects of the work for the rest of us to learn from. Caution enters because this discussion does not take up what should be done next or what might have been done better but rather attempts to join with the original author in opening up additional aspects of this exemplary situation.

I have attempted to write about working with psychotic patients (Muller, 1996) in a manner that relates the patient's anxiety to some Lacanian notions of psychotic processes. In 1946 Lacan stated, “Not only can our being not be understood without madness, it would not be our being if it did not bear madness within itself as the limit of its freedom” (1966, p. 176, my translation). In 1958 Lacan restated his position, adding, “What I am saying here is that it is the business of reason to recognize the drama of madness, … because it is in our relation to the signifier that this drama is situated” (1977, p. 214). I take “the signifier” here to be a metonymy for the entire symbolic order, the semiotic context of language, ritual, social custom, gesture, and sign, out of which signifiers arise to mediate aspects of human experience. Lacan's shorthand for this vast domain of language and culture is “the Other,” the “locus in which is constituted the I who speaks to him who hears” (1977, p. 141)

John Muller, Ph.D., is Director of Psychotherapy, Education, and Psychology Training at the Austen Riggs Center, Stockbridge, MA.

© 1998 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

Symposium on Clinical Judgment in Relational Psychoanalysis: The Problem of Technique: Introduction

Spyros Orfanos ABPP

Spyros Orfanos

303 Second Avenue, Suite 5
New York, NY 10003

Spyros Orfanos  

There are many experiences of contradiction in the practice of psychoanalysis. One of the most curious and problematic is the contradiction between, on one hand, the humanistic value placed on the unique and distinctive aspects of an analysis and, on the other hand, the analyst's desire to know and possess technical skill that is used across analytic cases. “Your technique should flow from your theoretical concepts,” instructed an early psychoanalytic supervisor of mine. She was responding to my desire to know what to do in a specific clinical situation. With youthful hubris, I told her I thought I knew what was going on, and what I wanted to know was exactly how I should phrase what I had to say to the patient. Her theory of technique stemmed from the Freudian notion that, if one understood the theory of mental functioning, then one could account for the patient's behavior and use any particular technique appropriate. In more recent times, the emphasis might be on developing techniques based on the particular dyad and its interpersonal and subjective natures.

Feelings of security and mastery often accompany the development of psychoanalytic technique, but at times these feelings also come with a sense of uneasiness. Confronted with similar clinical situations, we might worry about transplanting a phrase or behavior or attitude and thereby infringing on the unique, interactive matrix.

Spyros D. Orfanos, Ph.D., ABPP is Co-Director of the Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis. He is President-Elect of the Division of Psychoanalysis (39) of the American Psychological Association.

© 1998 The Analytic Press

Reply to Commentary

Philip Ringstrom Psy.D.

Philip Ringstrom

5004 Haskell Avenue
Encino, CA 91436
email: philsite@cyberese.net

Philip Ringstrom  

Ideeply appreciate Slavin and Kriegman's respectful, incisive, and largely supportive discussion of my article. We are in agreement that much of what they and I have proposed complement each other. Although I would like to say more about this, limitations of space, force me to address what appear to be four areas of questions and theoretical concerns regarding my article.

Should the Double Bind Paradigm Be Rooted in an Explanatory Theory?

Slavin and Kriegman assert that the double bind paradigm should be rooted in a “broader [theoretical] model of the human relational world and mind (that) guide our explanations of the phenomena that the language of double bind often so well describes.” It is my contention that this rooting of the double bind paradigm in a broader theoretical model is not essential or even necessarily desirable. I believe that both papers, theirs and mine, underscore the fact that impasses are often directly related to “hard spots” (McLaughlin, 1992) that emanate from loyalty to one metapsychological (explanatory) theory or another.

As Slavin and Kriegman themselves underscore, in their commentary, the double bind paradigm illustrated in my article, enables one to “achieve a way of steering a course through those wrenching moments that repeatedly challenge a therapist's sense of hope … (especially) … when theoretical loyalty fail[s] to provide adequate guidance [to] inevitable—perhaps existential—dilemma[s] that must be mutually endured and recognized in the course of fostering the patient's development.”

Symposium on the British Independents: Introduction

Anthony Bass PHD

Anthony Bass

27 West 72nd Street, #808
New York, NY 10023
email: alb250@aol.com

Anthony Bass  

Paul Williams's essay was the 1995 winner of the Rosenfeld Clinical Essay prize of the British Psychoanalytical Society, inaugurated in the memory of Dr. Herbert Rosenfeld, whose seminal psychoanalytical work with psychotic, borderline, and severely narcissistic patients helped widen the scope of psychoanalytic praxis worldwide. Analyzed by Klein, Rosenfeld is also well known for his extensive writings on the creative use of countertransference in working with severely disturbed patients. The prize is awarded every three years to a member of the British Psychoanalytical Society for an essay on original clinical psychoanalytic work with borderline or psychotic patients. Mr. Williams is himself a psychoanalyst affiliated with the Middle, or Independent, group within the British Society, as are British discussants Harold Stewart and Mary Twyman. Arthur Hyatt Williams is a long-standing member of the Kleinian group in London. We thought it would be timely and illuminating to organize a clinical and theoretical discussion among American and British analysts that would focus on the unusual and fascinating clinical work that Williams describes in his essay.

The British Middle Group was formed following the “Controversial Discussions,” the name given to the formal debates between Kleinian and (Anna) Freudian analysts in London in 1943 and 1944 that were sponsored by the British Psychoanalytical Society. The often vitriolic discussions eventuated in a decision to split the training program at the British Society into two parallel courses of study, an “A” group for candidates wishing to become Kleinian analysts, and a “B” group for those partial to the teachings of Anna Freud. A third group, called the Middle, or Independent, group, consisted of analysts in training who preferred to pursue seminar work and supervision as their interests moved them, uncommitted to one of the two major courses of study. Reply to Commentary

Michael Bader DMH

Michael Bader

2345 California Street
San Francisco, CA 94115
e-mail: drbader@aol.com

Michael Bader  

Iwant to thank Charles Spezzano for his thoughtful, fair, and generous response to my paper. While I duly noted his many complimentary remarks about the substance of my position, I especially appreciated the parallel with Jack Nicholson and was thankful that it was to his role in A Few Good Men and not The Shining!

Spezzano does two things, however, in his essay that leave the reader with the false impression that, although psychoanalysis needs me on that wall fighting for therapeutic outcome, my position tends to be intolerant of diversity, mechanical and overly categorical, and blind to the ways that the postmodernists are actually my fellow-travellers. First, he argues that all postmodernism wants is to add another kind of reasoning (i.e., reasoning poetically, imaginatively, through joint self-disclosure and the like) to my version of empiricism and hypothesis testing. Constructivist theory only informs and doesn't replace other epistemologies. He agrees with Renik that “there is nothing intrinsic in a constructivist-informed account of the analytic situation that links directly to a less empirical attitude.” The postmodernist doesn't eschew empiricism. Postmodernists simply go about their empirical business in a different way than I do, that is, by using different sources of information such as the analyst—patient dyad. Spezzano is presenting us with a kinder, gentler postmodernism.

My second objection is that Spezzano mistakenly characterizes my position in such a way as to make it seem rather mechanical and rigid. In his view, I seem to use observable data only from the patient, not the dyad, to formulate my hypotheses. My goal is to categorize interventions and patients and then link them together. I apparently insist on one kind of case write-up or clinical literature.

Michael Bader is a psychoanalyst practicing in San Francisco and is a contributing editor of Tikkun Magazine.

© 1998 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Reply to Commentary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentary

F. Barth M.S.W., C.S.W.

F. Barth

102 West 85th Street, #5H
New York, NY 10024

F. Barth  

As I noted at the beginning of “speaking of feelings,” the very act of putting ideas into words can convey a false sense of clarity and closure—which is in fact a common complaint of many of the analysands who inspired this discussion. Let me say immediately, therefore, that it was by no means my intention to suggest that attention to details of daily life outside of the therapy should always supplant examination of the moment-to-moment details of the analytic relationship even in an analysis of the most disturbed or fragile individual. In her thoughtful and provocative discussion, Dr. Brisman has elucidated an extremely important aspect of the process of working with analysands to articulate internal experience. There is no question that what Brisman describes as detailed attention “to what the patient is actually doing in the room, both with behaviors and with language,” is crucial to this activity. This interest, however, is not necessarily as straightforward as Brisman seems to be suggesting. Although there are many times when direct examination of the experience within the analysis is the most productive way to proceed, I have also found that—at times precisely because of the fears of contradicting and/or separating from the omniscient Other that Brisman describes—there are many occasions in which an analysand cannot tolerate the focus on the minute details of their verbal and nonverbal behavior in the analytic relationship.

My concern, however, is that these different approaches not be polarized into an either-or stance. Although there are growing numbers of theorists who suggest that examination of the workings of the analytic dyad are central to all analytic work, I believe that there are some serious dangers attached to a model that implies that this is the exclusive domain for analytic exploration. One of the difficulties concomitant with most contemporary psychoanalytic theorizing is our growing realization that the exploration of meaning often occurs without a clear map to guide us (Aron, 1997, elaborates beautifully on many of the dilemmas inherent in this trend in psychoanalytic thought).

Commentary on Paper by Paul Williams

Harold Stewart F.R.C.Psych

Harold Stewart

16 Ferncroft Avenue
London NN3 7 PH
England

Harold Stewart  

Paul Williams has presented us with an excellent portrayal of the treatment of a severely disturbed female patient, in which, for long periods of time, he was confronted with the problems of an analysis of a state of mind in which the psychotic part of the personality is in the ascendancy over the nonpsychotic. The patient had created a dissociated, projected, delusional pseudo-object to govern her thoughts and actions as a protection against the panic of confusion, fragmentation, and annihilation. Here I comment on my experiences with similar patients who were not so much borderline as psychotic, in which the same types of problems apply but to a different degree.

For these patients, I have found it necessary to have them in four-or five-times-a-week analysis and to maintain a firm setting in order to hold and contain them. The maintenance of boundaries and limits represents a crucial aspect of treatment. The analysis, as Williams indicated, is particularly of the here-and-now of the transference and occasionally of the there-and-then when the relevant past is known. This is more easily said than done, because the hating of both external and internal reality together with the denial of its recognition makes secondary process or sane thought extremely difficult. In these patients, there are delusional objects that may be dissociated and projected and experienced as coming from external sources, as with the “Director,” or that may be internalized and experienced as different personalities. For example, a patient of mine knew he was God or Moses or even an ancient rock in the desert. As Williams has indicated, these delusional objects have to be addressed by the analyst as independent objects without attempting to integrate them into the nonpsychotic aspect of the personality as split-off parts and thereby creating anxiety or compliance. [[RunningHead]]

Psychotic Questing for Heroism: Commentary on Paper by Paul Williams

Ann-Louise Silver MD

Ann-Louise Silver

Chestnut Lodge Hospital—CPC Health
500 W. Montgomery Avenue
Rockville, MD 20850
e-mail: ASILVER@psychoanalysis.net

Ann-Louise Silver  

Paul William's paper documents the heroic analytic treatment of a profoundly ill patient. Initially Williams, bombarded with disconnected images and affects, struggled to find firm mental ground. Together, he and his patient moved earth around, creating an island in this sea of confusion. With a different therapist, a different island would form. Williams stayed with Miss A without rebelling against her attacks on his thinking, forcing his reality on her, or abandoning her as “unanalyzable.” I sensed an instantaneous maternal transference, in which Miss A desperately tried to get her mother's (Williams's) attention (Kramer, 1996). Chronically, Williams had to contain his apprehension, reassuring himself that, if she had survived this long, she would probably be there for the next appointment.

Most impressively, Williams remained curious about how his patient understood his remarks. Rather than relying on “Let's see what comes to mind” and assuming his words were understood, Williams found that “exploring the fate of [his] interpretations was [his] main activity.” This provided an alternative to the “Director's” dogmatism. Rather than hearing “Do as I command,” Miss A heard, “I made an observation. I recognize that a powerful part of you blocks you from listening. Did you understand me? What is your opinion?” Gradually incorporating this attribute, she could negotiate with her own warring mental components. Thus, Williams demonstrated that there are better defenses against anxiety than dissociation.

Williams did not react fearfully, even when Miss A came armed with razor blades or knives. She sought praise for her bravery but found

Dr. Silver is Director of Education at Chestnut Lodge Hospital, Rockville, MD; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences, Bethesda, MD; and a faculty member at Washington Psychoanalytic Institute, Washington School of Psychiatry, and Walter Reed Army Medical Center. She is President-Elect of the American Academy of Psychoanalysis.

Michael Bader's (1998) essay seems important to me. To evaluate its importance, we have to recognize that his argument is not simply about being empirical but about being empirical in a very specific way: Form hypotheses, based on an understanding of the patient's psychology, about what the best intervention will be at the moment; try the intervention; observe the patient's reactions for signs that the intervention was analytically useful or not; don't leave any of that out when writing up the case; create a literature in which we accumulate patient-specific criteria for guiding our choice of interventions; don't write papers about interventions or ways of working that are not linked to these patient-specific criteria; don't imply that we cannot do any of the above; accept certain of the patient's responses to an intervention as adequate evidence that the analyst's hypotheses that informed the intervention constitute true understanding of the patient's psychology; stop “privileging” the analyst's inner experience and get on with the work of observing how our interpretations affect the patient; don't say you want to privilege both the analyst's subjectivity and the patient's subjectivity. responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who's Afraid of Post-Modernism?: Commentary on Paper by Michael J. Bader

Owen Renik MD

Owen Renik

244 Myrtle Street
San Francisco, CA 94109

Owen Renik  

In many ways, Michael is arguing on the side of the angels. I think he's right to object to the idealization of ambiguity and uncertainty, to deplore the decline of therapeutic aims in psychoanalysis, and to assert the importance of validation in theory-building. At the same time, I have questions about some aspects of his critique of contemporary trends in psychoanalytic thinking.

Michael believes that the current interest in how psychoanalytic understanding is socially constructed and in the intersubjective nature of the clinical analytic encounter encourages neglect of the disciplined practice of generating and testing hypotheses. He sees this “postmodern” turn as part of a broader cultural withdrawal—away from active engagement in the hope of social change, into cynical self-preoccupation.

It seems to me that there have always been psychoanalysts who enjoy playing with ideas in a way that has little connection to the experience of clinical work and offers no help to an analytic practitioner. Although there are certainly social constructivists and intersubjectivists whose work I find overly academic, I don't think Michael successfully makes the case that social constructivism and an intersubjective perspective per se dispose away from a pragmatic, empirical orientation. My own impression, on the contrary, is that social constructivism and an intersubjective perspective serve to enhance the pragmatic, empirical power of psychoanalytic theory, bringing to it methodological improvements analogous to the ones Einstein initiated in physics 80 years ago and increasing its clinical utility.

Dr. Renik is Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; and Editor-in-Chief, Psychoanalytic Quarterly.

© 1998 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentaries

Michael Bader DMH

Michael Bader

2345 California St.
San Francisco, CA 94115
E-mail: drbader@aol.com

Michael Bader  

Owen Renik

The reason that I am “arguing with the angels,” as Owen puts it, is that the angels need the help. The tendency in psychoanalysis to argue from theory and received wisdom rather than empirical observation and actual patient-specific outcomes has always been our field's Achilles heel, generating a host of so-called principles of technique that have led us to assume unnatural roles and treat patients in bizarre ways (e.g., analytic abstinence). The more bizarre we appear to be, the more we claim a special knowledge that justifies setting us apart from other helping professions. We cannot demonstrate that we actually help anybody better than nonanalytic practitioners do, so we act as if that shouldn't matter because our theory is deeper, more subtle, and more complex than that of those other folks. The more we are ridiculed by a popular culture preoccupied with quick-fix cures, the more we comfort ourselves with the notion that “they” don't appreciate the power of the unconscious like we do.

It is in this context that I examined the postmodern critique of positivism and empiricism in psychoanalysis. What I found was old wine in new wine skins. There is, on one hand, a new appreciation of the ways that the analyst's participation, both theoretical and personal, affects the clinical field. On the other hand, the critique of positivism tends to mystify how analysts understand their patients. By turning the process of intersubjective empathy, inference, and understanding into an almost infinitely complex endeavor, enshrouded in ambiguity and uncertainty, postmodern epistemology distracts us from developing clinical methodologies that use observable patient-specific data as guideposts to improve our understanding and therapeutic efficacy. In this sense, the postmodernists perpetuate the tendency to neglect therapeutic aims in psychoanalysis (Bader, 1994).

© 1998 The Analytic Press

61

Passion's Friends, Passion's Enemies: Commentary on Paper by Stephen A. Mitchell**

Ruth Stein PHD

Ruth Stein

3 Brody Street
Jerusalem, Israel 92502
email: rustein@netvision.net.il

Ruth Stein  

Stephen Mitchell's (1997) remarkable paper is a well-argued appeal to revive love and romance, which, in his view, had been degraded both in psychoanalysis and in the lives of many people, and to bring them to a more central place in psychoanalysis through a call to heal the “splitting between love and desire, the longing for inaccessible objects, the numbing of passion” (p. 27). The essay focuses in particular on the fading of romance and passion in long-standing relationships, marital or other, that are apparently stable and safe. Mitchell suggests that such relationships are in truth in a state of pending, above an abyss formed by the unpredictability and irrecuperability of the other, an abyss that as a rule is being collusively denied by both partners. It is in the nature of such relationships to turn romantic passion into a risky enterprise, a danger to be avoided if exposure to the menace of painful betrayal, abandonment, or withdrawal by a most important other is to be warded off. Mitchell offers us this unusual and unexpected hypothesis as a correction to and replacement for the old view that familiarity breeds passionlessness. In this, Mitchell touches on the issue of the complex relation between attachment and desire, safety and the erotic. He admits that reconciling safety and passion is difficult because “there is a fundamental contrast between the familiar and the transcendent that runs throughout human experience” (p. 27). However, he proposes, in a striking tour de force, that the familiar in familiar relationships is essentially a well-contrived screen for the strangeness and unpredictability of the other that lurk in these relationships to a far greater degree than in relationships with

Ruth Stein, Ph.D., is a Training and Supervising Analyst, the Israel Psychoanalytic Society and a lecturer at Tel Aviv University. She is on the International Editorial Board of Gender and Psychoanalysis and on the Editorial Board of American Journal of Psychotherapy.

Projective Identification Begone!: Commentary on Paper by Susan H. Sands**

Robert Stolorow PHD , Donna Orange PSYD and George Atwood PHD

George Atwood

11726 San Vicente Blvd., Ste. 410
Los Angeles, CA 90049

1270 Fifth Avenue, Apt. 11J
New York, NY 10029

20 Haver Farm Road
Clinton, NJ 08809

George Atwood  

In her recent article, Sands (1997a) seeks to cure self psychology's “phobic” opposition to projective identification by reformulating the concept “in terms that are more experience-near to self psychologists” (p. 652). From her extensive citations of our work, we infer that we are prominent among the phobics she wishes to disabuse of such aversive attitudes. Let us make our position clear from the start of our reply. We have never objected to the concept of projective identification because it is incompatible with self psychology. We object to it because we think it is bad theory. Before presenting our argument, however, there is another matter we must address. Despite being a thoughtful, knowledgeable, and respectful student and critic of our work, Sands seriously mischaracterizes our ideas in at least three instances.

Throughout her article, Sands repeatedly amalgamates our intersubjective perspective with self psychology, as if the two theories were one. They are not. In several publications now (e.g., Stolorow, 1992; Stolorow and Atwood, 1992; Atwood and Stolorow, 1993; Orange, Atwood, and Stolorow, 1997)

Robert D. Stolorow, Ph.D. is Faculty, Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles; Faculty, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UCLA School of Medicine.

Donna M. Orange, Ph.D., Psy.D. is Faculty and Supervising Analyst, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, New York City.

George E. Atwood, Ph.D. is a Founding Faculty Member, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, New York City, and Professor of Psychology, Rutgers University.

*Psychoanalytic Dialogues, Vol. 7, No. 5, 1997.

Having a Mind of One's Own and Holding the Other In Mind: Commentary on Paper by Peter Fonagy and Mary Target

Susan Coates PHD

Susan Coates

205 West 89th Street
New York, NY 10024-1838
E-Mail swcoates@mindspring.com

Susan Coates  

Peter Fonagy and Mary Target's contribution, “Mentalization and the Changing Aims of Child Psychoanalysis,” is a remarkable and important article. Fonagy and Target are continuing to report on an important body of programmatic research that they and their collaborators, notably George Moran and Miriam and Howard Steele, have been producing for more than a decade (Fonagy, 1991; Kennedy and Moran, 1991; Fonagy, Steele, et al., 1993; Fonagy et al., 1995; Fonagy and Target, 1995; Fonagy and Target, 1996). Their work is a part of a broad effort within psychoanalysis to integrate modern developmental research and thought into the psychoanalytic theory of development (Stern, 1985; Lyons-Ruth, 1991; Silverman, 1991; Slade and Aber, 1992; Diamond and Blatt, 1994; Main, 1995; Osofsky, 1995; Mayes and Cohen, 1996; Beebe, Lachmann, and Jaffe, 1997).

Dr. Coates is a Faculty Member at the Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research at Columbia University and is an Associate Clinical Professor of Medical Psychology in the Department of Psychiatry, College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University.

I thank Barbara Eisold, Ph.D., for her efforts in helping to bring about this integration by initiating and coordinating a conference in 1996 on Psychoanalysis & Attachment Theory sponsored by the Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis at New York University and Division 39. I would also like to thank Cassandra Cook, John Kerr, and Mary Sue Moore for their helpful comments on an earlier draft of this commentary.

From Ghosts to Ancestors: The Psychoanalytic Vision of Hans Loewald

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

251 West 71st Street
New York, NY 10023

Stephen Mitchell  

The papers of Hans Loewald are among the most frequently cited and least understood within the psychoanalytic literature. Part of the problem is that his prescient ideas, extraordinarily creative and often visionary, are presented in traditional terminology. This paper is intended as a contribution to the explication of Loewald's important contribution, focusing on his novel understanding of language (both the development of language in the child and the language of psychoanalysis), and his richly dialectical view of the relationship between fantasy and actuality.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Emergence of Features of the Analyst's Life

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

251 W. 71st Street
New York, NY, 10023
email: s.mitchell@psychoanalysis.net

Stephen Mitchell  

One of the issues that is a constant accompaniment to my clinical experience concerns the use I am to make of aspects of my own life, my past, my character traits, and the like as they become activated at different times with different patients. It concerns much more than countertransference “disclosure,” a legalistic term I have become increasingly unhappy with. These are more complex questions like: Why is this piece of my life emerging into my experience or, perhaps, the patient's experience at this time? Is the presence of this experience of mine likely to advance the analytic process at this point, or will it take us on a detour? How long do I follow it before deciding? Is it useful to make some or all of what I am experiencing explicit to the patient?

What follows are two clinical vignettes, one involving an initial consultation, the other an event well into an analysis, perhaps heralding the commencement of a move toward termination. I would like to to consider them here together, as greatly pared down snapshots, because they both involved the same feature of my own history. In the first, I decided not to speak of my personal associations with the patient; in the second I decided that I would.

George, a 35-year-old architect who is seeing several different analysts for consultations, enters the office for the first appointment with a jaunty air and a twinkle in his eye. Without requiring an invitation, he launches into a brisk, well-organized, and entertaining narrative of his life.

George is seeking treatment because he feels the approach of middle age and has a sense that there is considerable unfinished business left over from his childhood.

Dr. Mitchell is a Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute and is on the faculty of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. He is also editor of this journal.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gender Repertoire: Body, Mind, and Bisexuality

Dianne Elise PHD

Dianne Elise

5435 College Avenue, #106
Oakland, California 94618

Dianne Elise  

Selected aspects of case material in the treatment of a bisexual woman are used to illustrate the theme of penetrability versus impenetrability in the feminine and masculine psyches. I examine the dimensions not of activity and passivity, but of a fixed versus a permeable bodily and psychic boundary—the ability to penetrate as well as the ability to be penetrated. The patient showed shifting gender identifications, depending on the relational context—the geography of the lover's body and mind—that she described as “expanding her gender repertoire.” Bisexuality is discussed as a creative use of potential space that does not necessitate the collapse of core gender identity. An integration is offered of a clinical and theoretical focus on sexuality with an emphasis on earliest object relations. To the metaphors of the primal scene and the combined parent, I add the metaphor of the nursing couple as the site of bisexual identifications and as the earliest relation of the penetrating to the penetrated. I critique the gender split regarding the ability to penetrate—an ability not inherently male but that comes to be seen as such.

Irwin Hoffman  

“‘What if we do this,’ I suggested, ‘what if we don't close the door, but sit down just inside it, together? We won't go any further in … we'll just sit at the door and look inside and see if we can figure out what's so dangerous.’” In this evocative, poetic way, Jody Davies invites her patient David to explore with her the place of sexuality in their relationship at that moment. I believe that the psychoanalytic community, particularly its more progressive relational wing, bears a relationship to Davies much like David's. We are ready for something more and yet very hesitant and afraid to go further. And Davies has emerged as one of our most courageous and informed guides. She's at the frontier and has a sense of how it can be done, of how we can elaborate the simple truth that we have been confronting increasingly—namely, that the patient and the analyst are both human beings—to include the fact that they are both sexual beings. Davies challenges us, as she challenges David (in response, to be sure, to his cues and his bold initiative—“Forgive me if I'm wrong, but I think you're flirting with me”) to see and make constructive use of our own growing maturity as a discipline. He is on the editorial boards of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis and of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, and is the author of Ritual and Spontaneity in the Psychoanalytic Process: A Dialectical-Constructivist View (The Analytic Press, 1998).

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Choices and the Theory of Psychoanalytic Technique: Commentary on Papers by Mitchell and by Davies

Lewis Aron PHD

Lewis Aron

13 Longview Road
Port Washington, NY 11050-3039
email: lewaron@psychoanalysis.net

Lewis Aron  

Lecturing in Rio De Janeiro in 1974, Wilfred Bion—who was by then 77 years old, a former President of the British Psychoanalytic Society, and one of the most original and influential thinkers in the psychoanalytic world (not to mention what may, in fact, be most relevant, that he had been a World War I tank commander and genuine military hero)—was asked a series of questions about the handling of fundamental technical matters. Do you ask patients questions, or do you wait for free associations? Are you willing to speak with the relatives of your patients? Would you visit a patient who had to be hospitalized? I want to begin and end this article with portions of Bion's reply, which are ironic, coming from someone who spent half his life charting the elements of psychoanalysis and ruling over the psychoanalytic establishment. “In the practice of psychoanalysis,” he responded, “it is difficult to stick to the rules. For one thing, I do not know what the rules of psycho-analysis are” (Bion, 1990, p. 139). Now, of course, these comments may be dismissed by some as the ramblings of an old man who should have already retired, but I think that there is much wisdom in his response.

Let's begin with Steve Mitchell's remark that the great value of classical technique was that it helped the clinician decide what to do.

Lewis Aron, Ph.D., ABPP is Clinical Professor and Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. He is Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues and the author of A Meeting of Minds (The Analytic Press, 1996).

Old and New Objects in Fairbairnian and American Relational Theory

Steven Cooper PHD and David Levit PHD

David Levit

875 Massachusetts Avenue
Cambridge, MA 02139

875 Massachusetts Avenue
Cambridge, MA 02139

David Levit  

In this paper we explore a few points emphasized by both Fairbairn and American Relational Theory related to conceptualizations of the new and old object and the implications for working in the analytic situation. Underlying much of our discussion is the belief that there are useful tensions between, on the one hand, Fairbairn's emphasis on our attachment to bad and old objects and refractory clinging to these bad objects in the face of receiving something new, and, on the other, a construal of a push or willingness toward integrating externality and newness of objects (e.g., Winnicott, 1969; Benjamin, 1988). American Relational models which explore complexities of interaction augment an understanding of Fairbairn's formulations of why we hold on to old objects. Fairbairn's theory also offers ways of understanding aspects of repetition, even, and sometimes especially, in trying to provide new experience.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Multiple Perspectives on Multiplicity

Jody Davies PHD

Jody Davies

441 West End Avenue, Ste. 2C
New York, NY 10024

Jody Davies  

In the clinical material presented here, I wish to address that aspect of relational theory that has begun to conceive of self, indeed of mind itself, as a multiply organized, associationally linked network of parallel, coexistent, at times conflictual, systems of meaning attribution and understanding. I hope to show how dissociation, even when conceived of as the overarching organizational mental process, always exists in a state of intimate tension with associational processes. Such an organization of self and of the multiple selves that constitute “mind” implies the carefully balanced maintenance of an optimal tension state between the primarily memory-based connections that give us a sense of psychic integrity and the dynamic processes that threaten to splinter our internal organization; a kind of counter-balancing of centrifugal and centripedal forces, allowing the parts to function as a system in well-established patterns of psychic meaning but threatening at times to fly out of control into chaos.

Elsewhere (Davies, 1996a, b) I have compared this way of understanding mind to the image and structure of a child's kaleidoscope. Multitudinous yet finite organizations of substructures, always shifting, yet always moving in relationship to one another; patterns, at first novel, becoming identifiable over time and with repeated exposure. Such repetitive patterns moving in and out of positions of prominence but reappearing with regularity and predictability, patterns representing our most significant formative relationships, provide a sense of character and agency, an experience that we are knowable to ourselves and others. Such patterns create meaning out of chaos, providing the lenses through which we view the world and interpret what happens to us.

Jody Messler Davies, Ph.D. is Supervisor and Cochair of the Relational Track, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; Faculty and Supervisor, National Institute for the Psychotherapies; and Associate Editor, Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

[[RunningHead]]

The Play's the Thing How the Essential Processes of Therapy Are Seen Most Clearly in Child Therapy

Jay Frankel PHD

Jay Frankel

290 West 12th Street, #6B
New York, NY 10014

Jay Frankel  

Analytic child therapy techniques developed as modifications of techniques from adult psychoanalysis. Child therapy continues to be regarded as an adaptation of adult analysis and to give a central place to the methods and conditions of adult analysis, such as interpretation, in its understanding of how therapy heals. I propose that child therapy is not a modified form of therapy and that the essential processes of therapy are fully present in child therapy. In fact, they often may be seen more clearly there than in adult therapy. I suggest two interrelated processes as the essential ones in all analytic therapy. The first is play. I examine several interrelated aspects of play, specifically as they occur in child therapy. These include the emergence and integration of dissociated self-states, symbolization, and recognition. The second process I propose as essential in analytic therapy is the renegotiation of self-other relationships through action. This renegotiation is what can help patients become able to play in therapy when they have difficulty doing so. Since I suggest that action is at the heart of analytic therapy, I go on to consider the role of talking in an action therapy. Finally, I explore the dimensions of mutuality in the relationship between child and therapist,

He is also on faculty at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and the Institute for Contemporary Psychotherapy, both in New York, and the Contemporary Center for Advanced Psychoanalytic Studies, in New Jersey. He also supervises in the Child and Adolescent Psychotherapy Training Programs at the National Institute for the Psychotherapies and the William Alanson White Institute, both in New York.

Earlier versions of parts of this article were presented at the spring meetings of the Division of Psychoanalysis of the American Psychological Association in March 1992 in Philadelphia, and April 1996 in New York.

responseSet.title: Patients' Unconscious Plans for Solving Their Problems
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Patients' Unconscious Plans for Solving Their Problems

Joseph Weiss MD

Joseph Weiss

2420 Sutter Street
San Francisco, CA 94115

Joseph Weiss  

The idea that people plan their behavior is considered common sense for most human activities. This commonsense view, however, is not extended to the behavior of the patients in therapy. Patients' behavior is thought to be governed to some extent by unplanned unconscious forces. In this paper, I challenge that view. I assume that the primary motive of patients in psychotherapy, including psychoanalysis, is to solve their problems by disproving the pathogenic beliefs that underlie them, and it assumes, too, that patients make and carry out plans for solving their problems. Patients' plans are for testing their pathogenic beliefs with the therapist in the hope that the therapist will not react to their tests as their beliefs predict. In research studies carried out by the San Francisco Psychotherapy Research Group, we demonstrated that we can reliably infer patients' plans from their behavior at the beginning of therapy and that patients work consistently in accordance with these plans throughout the therapy. Support for this thesis appears in infant research, evolutionary psychology, academic cognitive research, and linguistics. The view of the therapeutic process proposed here bears on the current discussion of the therapist's use of authority and integrates several current trends in the theory of therapy.

Glen Gabbard  

In a series of creative, provocative, and carefully argued contributions, Jody Davies has explored the largely uncharted territory of the analyst's erotic experience. Until quite recently, the literature on the erotic tension in the analytic setting was written almost entirely by male analysts about their experiences with female patients. In this context, Davies's contributions have been particularly welcome, as they have broken ground in illuminating the sexual subjectivity of the female analyst. Thus, it is a privilege and a pleasure to once again be invited to discuss Davies's latest contribution.

Arguing that analysts have tended to collapse all forms of sexual excitement in the analytic dyad into a form of infantile, usually oedipal, sexuality, Davies stresses that we have not done justice to the phenomenon of normal adult sexuality as it is manifested in clinical practice. I certainly share her concern about the relative neglect of postoedipal erotic experience. It is ironic that, in the midst of society's alarm at the prevalence of childhood sexual abuse, the subject of how to introduce adolescents to healthy sexuality is almost entirely ignored. We can infer that somehow this transition is so suffused with taboos that there has been a collective withdrawal from this problem.

Like Davies, I have long argued that love and desire in the analytic situation cannot be reductionistically viewed as stemming from one developmental phase (Gabbard, 1996a, b). Endless debates about whether erotic transference represents genital-oedipal wishes, breakdown products of selfobject failure, or the sexualization of fusion wishes have not advanced our understanding of the clinical phenomena

Glen O. Between the Disclosure and Foreclosure of Erotic Transference-Countertransference: Can Psychoanalysis Find a Place for Adult Sexuality?

Jody Davies PHD

Jody Davies

441 West End Avenue, #2C
New York, NY 10024

Jody Davies  

In this article I explore the concept of a postoedipal adult sexuality and its role in the erotic transference-countertransference processes seen in psychoanalytic treatment. This concept challenges the fundamental assumption that, whenever erotic feelings enter the psychoanalytic space, the analyst always stands in the role of the oedipal parent. It suggests that clinical choices made from within an oedipal model often fail to recognize certain significant developmental changes. An extended clinical example is provided.

Mentalization and the Changing Aims of Child Psychoanalysis

Peter Fonagy PHD and Mary Target PHD

Mary Target

Gower
Street
London WC1E 6BT
E-mail: p.fonagy@ucl.ac.uk.

Mary Target  

The interface of empirical work with child psychoanalysis at the Anna Freud Centre is part of the tradition of systematic study and research pioneered over many years by Anna Freud and her colleagues (A. Freud, 1962; Sandler, 1962; A. Freud 1965).

George Moran initiated a program of work at the Anna Freud Centre, which, starting with the work on juvenile onset diabetes (Fonagy et al., 1991b), led us to ask fundamental questions about the nature of the child psychoanalysis, with important implications for technique. We acknowledged our debt by dedicating the lecture on which this paper is based to George Moran, whom we see as a worthy successor to Marianne Kris, both of them working within the tradition of Anna Freud.

This article starts with observations that imply the need for a change in some aspects of our psychoanalytic model. Throughout child psychoanalysis is used to illustrate both the need for change and the character of the revision that is required. The authors, however, assert that, as is so often the case for explorations in child analysis, these ideas may be extrapolated to psychoanalytic approaches to adults, specifically individuals with borderline personality disorder.

Peter Fonagy, Ph.D. is Freud Memorial Professor of Psychoanalysis, University College London; Director, Sub-Department of Clinical Health Psychology, University College London; Director of Research, Anna Freud Centre; and Co-ordinating Director, Child and Family Center and Center for Outcomes Research and Effectiveness, Menninger Foundation.

Mary Target, Ph.D. is Senior Lecturer, University College London, and Deputy Director of Research, Anna Freud Centre.

An earlier version of this article was presented as the 1997 Marianne Kris Memorial Lecture at the annual meeting of the Association for Child Psychoanalysis Inc., March 1997, Cancun, Mexico. The authors would like to acknowledge the vital contributions of Dr. Efrain Bleiberg and Drs. Miriam and Howard Steele to the thinking and empirical work that are drawn on in this article.

Staying the Same While Changing: Reflections on Clinical Judgment

Philip Bromberg PHD

Philip Bromberg

300 Central Park West

Philip Bromberg  

Having participated in more symposia than I care to remember, I've come to believe that regardless of whether an analyst is a writer or a discussant, the working definition of “clinical judgment” is always arrived at by hindsight—the writer defines it as what he did, and the discussant (no matter how sophisticated his efforts at tactfulness) defines it as what it would have been better to do. In this light, with apologies offered in turn to Jack Benny and Zero Mostel, I'm going to begin with a story that might be called “a funny thing happened to my title on the way to the symposium,” a story that turned out to be an unanticipated example of how I see the issue of clinical judgment.

When I received my copy of the agenda I discovered that the title of my paper read “Staying Sane While Changing,” not, the title I had submitted. It seemed that someone had already been exercising clinical judgment. In my fantasy, the “someone” was a copyeditor who found paradox difficult to appreciate. I could imagine the person looking at my actual title, “Staying The Same While Changing,” and saying to himself, “Bromberg couldn't really have meant that. A patient can't both stay the same and change. Ah, I know. I'll bet the title is ‘staying sane while changing,’ and his secretary heard him wrong.”

The most interesting part of the story to me (apart from the fantasy gratification of my real yearning for a secretary)

Ringstrom states that our perspective on negotiating conflict and deception/self-deception “finds a resonating chord” in his double bind model. He uses our case—Edward and his analyst—to illustrate how metacommunication concerning paradoxical binds was the fulcrum around which something “truly transformational” happened in that therapeutic relationship. We, too, feel an important “resonating chord” both in our shared sense of the inevitable impasses and binds in treatment and our sense that, in the process of dealing with these binds, the possibility of relational movement towards genuine intersubjectivity emerges (Aron, 1991; Benjamin, 1995). Yet, as we read him. Ringstrom also seems to imply that a) his emphasis on the paradoxical, mutually negating, aspects of the relationship between the patient's and analyst's needs differs fundamentally from our emphasis on the conflict between them (and within each of them)—conflict rooted in their divergent needs, and, b) his emphasis on loosening the double bind through metacommunication differs fundamentally from our emphasis on the process of internal and interactive negotiation.

We do not believe that the key differences in our views of the process of dealing with the inherent contradictions and binds in treatment entail such a dichotomous view of conflict versus paradox or of negotiation versus metacommunication. Charles Spezzano  

The philosopher Alphonso Lingis titled one of his books The Community of Those Who Have Nothing in Common. The label “relational” points to a community of psychoanalysts who, in their own view, might well come close to warranting a description like Lingis's. Reflecting on this in a conversation a few years ago, Rachael Peltz and I found ourselves drifting toward the idea that an independent school was forming in the United States. When, I introduced the phrase “American Middle School” (Spezzano, 1995), a number of readers commented that they welcomed such an ambiguous identity. At that time, I imagined this middle to stand between ego analysis and self psychology. Now I might picture it as the central overlapping area of a ring of circles, each of which represents one of the major schools of analytic literature from which American analysts draw inspiration and information: interpersonal psychoanalysis, Kleinian analysis, ego analysis, self psychology, and object relations theory, as well as Mitchell's relational hybrid, Stolorow's intersubjectivity, and Hoffman's constructivism—although looking at all those names, I might consider, and not with any shame or discontent, the label American Muddle School, for analysts who allow themselves to draw from several or all of these literatures.

Charles Spezzano, Ph.D., is a Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California. He is the author of What to Do Between Birth and Death: The Art of Growing Up (1993) and Affect in Psychoanalysis: A Clinical Synthesis (The Analytic Press, 1993).

Unconscious Plans and Unconscious Conflict: Reply to Commentary

Joseph Weiss MD

Joseph Weiss

2420 Sutter Street
San Francisco, CA 94115

Joseph Weiss  

Ithank Wachtel and DeMichele for their friendly discussion of my paper and for the interesting questions they raise. Their questions give me an opportunity to expand my ideas. I'll begin with their peripheral concerns and move toward their central concern—namely, their reservations about the plan concept. I'll also return to a discussion of Mrs. C.

As Wachtel and DeMichele point out, my perspective has been shaped by Freud's revision of the theory of anxiety in “Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety” (1926). Freud's revision of this theory changed the psychoanalytic concept of psychic conflict. Freud (1926) assumed such conflict is between a wish and a fear. A patient is afraid to pursue a particular goal because she unconsciously assumes that doing so is dangerous. In childhood, she perceived the danger as real and external. Later, she internalized it as a belief. Consider Freud's paradigmatic example. A male patient in childhood experienced as real and external the danger that he would be castrated for his sexuality, and so he became frightened of his sexuality and impeded in his pursuit of sexual goals. Subsequently, he internalized this danger by developing a belief in castration as a punishment. (Freud almost always wrote about castration anxiety as stemming from a belief and not from a fantasy.) Henceforth, the patient's conflict was internalized. He continued to pursue sexual goals and continued to be impeded in this pursuit by anxiety stemming from the belief in castration.

Freud's (1926) revision of theory paved the way for Alexander's conception of the corrective emotional experience (Alexander and French, 1946). If repression and hence psychopathology are held in place by a belief derived from experience, then new experiences that run counter to the belief may help the patient change the belief and so prove to be corrective.

[[RunningHead]]

Speaking of Feelings: Affects, Language, and Psychoanalysis

F. Barth M.S.W., C.S.W.

F. Barth

102 West 85th Street, #5H
New York, NY 10024

F. Barth  

For a surprising number of analysands, many with above-average intelligence and facility with language, words do not adequately capture or convey emotion or symbolize experience. This often subtle difficulty can have a powerful impact on the ability to verbally communicate emotions, process affect, and utilize traditional psychoanalytic interventions. Analysands with these problems therefore often have difficulty using analysis to explore and understand their own experience, yet the reasons for such difficulties are often not recognized by either analyst or analysand. In this article, I suggest that the concept of alexithymia can provide a valuable tool for understanding this gap between affects and language and, in its broadest interpretation, can aid therapists in their pursuit of meaningful approaches to the analytic process. Using the concept as a starting point, I describe and illustrate an approach in which paying attention to apparently insignificant aspects of an analysand's experience gradually helps analysands identify and explore personal symbols and meanings in their lives.

[[RunningHead]]

Finding the Way Out: Commentary on Papers by Malcolm Owen Slavin and Daniel Kriegman and by Philip A. Ringstrom**

Jessica Benjamin PHD

Jessica Benjamin

228 West 22nd Street
New York, New York 10011

Jessica Benjamin  

Slavin and Kriegman (1998a) write eloquently of the inevitability of conflict—of the psychic undertow in which each person tries to use the other and pull the other into his or her subjective world. Yet, their notion of individual interest recognizes interdependence—that each person must take in the other in order to construct her or his own subjectivity. I was particularly taken by the title of their paper, for I have often thought that for many people the point of entering therapy is to get the other to change. “Be my Other and change for me, because I have always been surrounded by immovable, impermeable objects upon whom I can have no impact, except by destroying them,” is the message of many a patient. In my thinking about intersubjectivity, which has much in common with what these authors describe, the inevitable collision with the different or opposing aims of the other, with the non-identity of one self to another, is the fundamental core of trouble in “subject relations”—even when, as the authors say, caregivers and therapists are good enough. However, this ineluctable blow to eogcentricism is also an opportunity to recognize the separate and equivalent center of the other. Through the other's survival and nonretaliatory assertion of his or her own identity, as Winnicott (1971) first pointed out, we find that there is someone else to love—or, as Stern (1985) suggests, that there are other minds out there in the universe.

The insight—that this fundamental condition should be conceived as distinct from, though intertwined with, biographical relational failures or intrapsychic conflicts—is important in delineating the area of intersubjectivity.

*Psychoanalytic Dialogues (1998), Vol. 8, No. 2.

Philip Ringstrom Psy.D.

Philip Ringstrom

5004 Haskell Ave.

Philip Ringstrom  

Despite his general contempt for philosophy and philosophers, perhaps the greatest legacy of Freud to Western civilization is his taking its cornerstone pursuit of truth out of the realm of rationality and pure reason and extending it to the realm of the irrational and the unconscious. In this way, even if unwittingly, he has endowed us with a “science of subjectivity” (Lear, 1995) to which we are all his grateful heirs. This pursuit of truth, finally wrestled from the realm of so-called objectivity, has returned us to the central admonition of Polonius, “This above all—to thine own self be true.” Is there any greater reason than this to put all the “blood, sweat, and tears” into the psychoanalytic endeavor?

I think that in the pairing of Slavin and Kriegman's paper with my own we see two sets of authors on the same journey, though perhaps coming at it from somewhat differing trajectories or at least semantical emphases, ones that may prove to create some subtle differences amidst a many of similarities. In both cases, we believe that, to a considerable extent, the paradoxical nature of the psychoanalytic enterprise is a byproduct of the participants' attempts to be true to themselves, even when that truth is unconsciously lost upon them. Psychotic Developments in a Sexually Abused Borderline Patient

Paul Williams PHD

Paul Williams

1/15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW35UJ
England
e-mail: 100576.1235@compuserve.com

Paul Williams  

The author describes the psychoanalysis of a female patient who was diagnosed psychiatrically as a paranoid psychotic. The patient reported a history of chronic sexual abuse. The psychopathology and the phases of treatment are considered, with particular reference to the dynamics and content of psychotic ideation and their transference and countertransference implications. In particular, the author points to the qualitative distinctiveness of psychotic thought, which, if mistaken for nonpsychotic thought, can generate confusion leading to impasse. Differentiation of the two levels of mentation and interpretation of their symbolic meaning in the nonpsychotic transference is necessary for the psychotherapy of psychosis to proceed.

[[RunningHead]]

When Actions Speak Louder Than Words: Verbal and Nonverbal Wrangling in the Therapeutic Arena: Commentary on Paper by F. Diane Barth

Judith Brisman PHD

Judith Brisman

24 East 12th Street, Ste 505
New York, NY 10003

Judith Brisman  

Patients' use of language in the analytic setting can undergo extreme and sometimes surprising fluctuations. Seemingly articulate and engaging patients retreat to the concrete in their use of words, disconnecting verbal expression from one's internal experience. Are these fluctuations indications of limitations in the patient's capacity to put experience into language, or are they indications of the emergence of otherwise unacknowledged aspects of self into the treatment arena? Shifts in the use of language (both patient's and therapist's) can be opportunities to question the work of the analysis. Whether one uses these moments to expand the boundaries of the analytic technique or to expand the boundaries of the analytic relationship—or both—is the question posed in this commentary.

Analysis to the Core: Commentary on Papers by James H. Hansell and Dianne Elise

Judith Butler PHD

Judith Butler

Berkeley, CA 94720-2670
jpbutler@socrates.berkeley.edu

Judith Butler  

These two very interesting essays seek to complicate developmental accounts of the formation of gender and sexual orientation. Although the developmental literature is somewhat new to me, I would like to raise a few questions about the kinds of explanations that are offered here, what we might expect from developmental accounts, and what kind of history they tell.

In a provocative and engaging essay, Hansell suggests at least two important theoretical points for the reconsideration of gender formation. First, he suggests that the repudiation of homosexuality that produces gendered melancholia within adult heterosexual men ought to be understood less as a repudiation of homosexual aim and object than as a compromise formation in which the aim is split off from its object. According to this latter view, the person to whom homosexual attachment has been made is not literally “lost,” and the homosexual desire for that person survives in the mode of disguise. Hansell argues that this form of disowning and disguising homosexual attachment actually allows for the satisfaction of homosexual desire under conditions that are not recognized to be fulfilling this function: “Much of what is forbidden and dangerous in any intrapsychic conflict is not forbidden impulses per se but the individual's conscious awareness of them.”

Judith Butler, Ph.D., is Chancellor's Professor, Departments of Rhetoric and Comparative Literature, University of California at Berkeley, and most recently author of The Psychic Life Power: Essays in Subjection (1997).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Clinical Moment

Jay Greenberg PHD

Jay Greenberg

275 Central Park West, #1B
New York, NY 10024

Jay Greenberg  

Francine, L, a 37-year-old woman, had been given my name by a colleague whom she had asked specifically for a referral to a therapist with an object relations orientation. Not a professional in our field, she had heard a bit about relational thinking and believed that it would be most helpful to her because of its concern with the real characteristics of peoples' parents and with what really went on in family interactions.

Ms. L was considerably underemployed as a low-level administrator. Although she had an impressive number of female friends, most were either married or on the verge of finding lasting relationships, and she felt increasingly isolated. Weekends were terrible; she had little to do, had trouble structuring her time, and was often unable, or at least unmotivated, to leave her apartment. Feeling anxious and despairing, sometimes she barely left her bed. These particularly distressing feelings, plus a growing sense that life was passing her by, had led her to seek treatment. She did not mention as an area of special concern that she was markedly obese, weighing in the area of 300 pounds.

During our initial consultation, Ms. L described a previous treatment with a prominent Freudian analyst. This therapy had gone on for five years and had been (from what I could gather) conducted along classical lines. Interpretations had focused on oedipal themes and on Ms. L's skill and frequent success at manipulating others. Through much of this time the patient felt bereft, misunderstood, incapable of getting through to this powerful, rejecting woman analyst. Frequently, after sessions, the patient would go into a stairwell outside the analyst's office and sit, curled up, crying for anywhere up to two hours. The analyst, who knew about this, interpreted it as the patient's manipulation.

Dr. Greenberg is a Training and Supervising Analyst, William Alanson White Institute; and Faculty and Supervising Analyst, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. He is the author of Oedipus and Beyond (1991) and editor of Contemporary Psychoanalysis.

Penetrating Psychoanalytic Theory: Reply to Commentary

Dianne Elise PHD

Dianne Elise

5435 College Avenue, #106
Oakland, California 94618

Dianne Elise  

Butler seeks an explanation as to why the “metaphysically vexed notion” of core gender identity continues to be valued. I agree with Butler that this concept has very much to do with the normalization of (certain) sexuality. I previously offered a critique of the theory of core gender identity as it specifically relates to females and to the concept of “primary femininity” (Elise, 1997). A complete answer to Butler's question—involving an explication of much of psychoanalytic history and theory—would require an effort beyond my abilities in this reply. This question has been artfully addressed, however, by Harris (1991) and Sweetnam (1996). Our current compromise seems to include recognition of the paradoxical need for both a fixed and a fluid sense of gender.

As for myself, core gender identity is a concept that I have encountered, not created or embraced, in my attempts to penetrate the psychoanalytic corpus with my own thinking. I need to enter the body (of literature) presented to me by my forebears in order to affect that body. Otherwise, I would be having theoretical intercourse with another body of thought, and my proposed partner would remain unmoved. Hopefully, my penetration will influence the shape of this particular body of knowledge (even if this works slowly in the way that water alters the contours of rock).

I believe we do learn something when we register in writing patients' expressions of their affective experiences. Our work as clinicians focuses us on the attempt to understand each individual psyche. The utterances of our patients are the matrix from which our theories are generated. Case material is invaluable in bringing theory alive both literally and figuratively. I concur with Butler that case examples do not prove anything. Psychotic Developments in a Sexually Abused Borderline Patient: Commentary on Paper by Paul Williams

Arthur Williams MD

Arthur Williams

29 St. Johns Road
London NW 11 OPE
England

Arthur Williams  

This is a first-class description of a psychoanalytical odyssey reaching over a period of seven years. The patient, Miss A, was aged 40 when she started psychoanalysis with Williams. She had been diagnosed as suffering from paranoid schizophrenia. She worked as an operations member of a branch of military security that involved recurrent risk of injury or loss of life. Her performance at her work was held in high regard by her seniors. It became apparent that her good work performance was poised precariously on the quicksand of a much-abused infancy and childhood and that there was serious turmoil “betwixt damnation and impassioned clay” going on all the time in every aspect of her life. She appears to have been grossly traumatized by her and father, who abused her from the age of five by masturbating her and getting her to practice fellatio on him. Later there was genital contact without penetration, but from the age of 10 there was penetration. This went on until she was 14, when she stopped it.

She had been rejected by her mother and more or less handed over to her father while her mother seemed to be besotted in her relationship with her first child, a boy a year and a half older than the patient. Five years later her younger brother was born. His birth seems to have coincided with the sexual deprivation of her father, who took on his daughter as a little wife. The five year-old was flattered and “honored” by this role but was pinned down in it for years. Refusal of father's overtures aroused his anger and sometimes violence. It is easy to see how a change toward more normal emotional development was impossible for the child. Her dangerous work later in life might very well have been both an expression of her problem and a lure to her. But it did not help her to cope with a highly split intrapsychic situation.

[[RunningHead]]

When Feeling Is Unbearable: Commentary on Paper by Paul Williams

Mary Twyman MA

Mary Twyman

14 Fitzjohn's Avenue
London NW35NA
England

Mary Twyman  

I was struck, in the first instance, by Paul Williams's observation on Bion's remark that an intrinsic gap exists between the analyst's knowledge and the patient's experience. He followed this comment by posing a question: “Is it possible that terms like ‘good’ object, ‘relationship,’ and ‘mother’ are the equivalents of anthropomorphisms when used in relation to a psychotic personality? In frank psychosis there is an asocial, inhuman quality of functioning for which ‘narcissism’ … is too advanced a term and superego … is commensurate with the dissociation involved.” This brought to mind the experience of a mother with a new baby in the first days, even weeks, of life; at this stage, the mother is used by the infant in a way that might approximate asocial, inhuman. It is as though the baby were living dominated by its impulses and awareness of the object is barely there. One might note the rooting response in its insistence, which is preparatory to the relationship with the breast. It seems to me that, through much of the analysis, Williams was faced with finding a way of being with his patient that encompassed continuous living with someone utterly embroiled in primitive, unfiltered experiences.

It is understandable that he speaks tellingly of the “post-hoc coherence of the paper.” It is a remarkable account of a most unusual treatment, bearing witness to both the vulnerability and resilience of the analyst: vulnerability, because the analyst is called on to expose in himself quite fearful (literally) areas of experience; resilience, because by his having done so, some hard-won contact became possible with the patient. In describing this in terms of vulnerability, I aim to draw attention to the particular quality of countertransference experience that such a patient arouses in the analyst.

Mary Twyman, M.A. is a psychologist of the British Psychological Society. She is in private practice in London..
