Unconscious Plan or Unconscious Conflict?: Commentary on Joseph Weiss's Paper

Paul Wachtel PHD and Annette DeMichele J.D.

Annette DeMichele

79 West 12th Street
New York, NY 10011

444 Central Park West, #7C
New York, NY 10025

Annette DeMichele  

The development of psychoanalysis as a discipline has been uneven. Advances in theory have not always been matched by advances in technique, and advances in one aspect of theory have not necessarily reverberated thoroughly throughout the body of psychoanalytic thought. In important ways, addressing this dissociation of influence is at the heart of the efforts to reform and reformulate a psychoanalytic approach to therapy undertaken by Weiss, Sampson, and the Mount Zion Psychotherapy Research Group (1986). Weiss's starting point is in the distinction between Freud's 1911-1915 theory and the ego psychology that evolved a decade later. He argues that the influence of the early theory persists, despite its having been superseded by Freud's later insights into mental functioning, and that many of the important developments of Freud's most mature theorizing still are insufficiently incorporated into the way analysts think about the therapeutic process. As a consequence, the element of resistance in the patient's behavior and his or her efforts to seek infantile gratification are exaggerated, and more progressive motivations and more mature and reality-oriented cognitive functioning are minimized.

We will return to this distinction on Weiss's part, but we begin with another divergence between “old” and “new” psychoanalysis that we believe bears equally significantly on the technical and theoretical changes Weiss proposes.

Paul L. Wachtel, Ph.D., is Distinguished Professor of Psychology, Doctoral Program in Clinical Psychology, City College, City University of New York.

Annette DeMichele, J.D., is an attorney and Advanced Doctoral Student in Clinical Psychology, City College, City University of New York.

[[RunningHead]]

The Dialectic of Presence and Absence: Impasses and the Retrieval of Meaning States

Rachael Peltz PHD

Rachael Peltz

3120 Telegraph, Ste. 1
Berkeley, CA 94705

Rachael Peltz  

Every analysis involves oscillations in the ability to generate new meaning, depending on the nature of the anxieties present and containable by the patient, the analyst, or both. Breakdown in this creative process can result in treatment impasses when primitive anxieties are uncontained and projective identifications abound. In this article, I situate clinical impasses within the dialectic of presence and absence.

That dialectic requires that the tension be sustained between both terms in such a way that neither is fully negated and a space can exist between the terms, a space in which meaning can flourish and the recognition of freedom can be embraced. I view impasses as psychic and philosophical breakdowns in which presence and absence are polarized and the ability to constitute presence out of absence is lost. I examine this collapsed space as one in which goodness has become perverted in the mind of the patient. I then offer clinical examples in which the analyst discovers how attention to her own subjective experiences helps to decipher the meaning of unconscious communications. These subjective experiences include the emergence of an internal supervisor, subliminal thought and images, and dreams about the patient. These communications can then serve as tools to release the analytic couple from the bonds of presymbolic treatment impasses.

Rachael Peltz, Ph.D., is Analyst and Supervisor, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, San Francisco, and Adjunct Faculty, Wright Institute, Berkeley. Since 1991, she has co-chaired the Committee on Social Responsibility of the American Psychological Association's Division of Psychoanalysis.

I thank the following people for their helpful suggestions and support in the writing of this essay: Elliott Currie, Sue Elkind, Zeese Papanikolas, Ruth Fallenbaum, Marsha Silverstein, Jed Sekoff, Ray Poggi, Maureen Katz, Mary Margaret McClure, Irwin Hoffman, Stuart Pizer, and Stephen Mitchell. I especially thank Thomas Ogden for his inspiration and encouragement over the years.

Earlier versions of this essay were presented at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, June 1995, and the San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute, September 1996.

Therapeutic Impasses in Contemporary Psychoanalytic Treatment: Revisiting the Double Bind Hypothesis

Philip Ringstrom Psy.D.

Philip Ringstrom

5004 Haskell Avenue
Encino, CA 91436
email: philsite@cyberesc.net

Philip Ringstrom  

In 1956, Bateson and colleagues published their seminal paper on double bind theory. Though long ago discounted as a truly etiological theory of schizophrenia, the double bind hypothesis left an enduring and unshakable impression, for perhaps what is most maddening in life are repeated encounters with feeling “damned if one does and damned if one doesn't.”

The double bind hypothesis, however, went much further than this colloquialism but, in so doing, often defied operational definition. In her review of the many attempts to empirically capture the double bind situation, Abeles (1976) concluded that investigators were “researching the unresearchable” (p. 113). Sluzki (1976) pointed out, however, that “what the group was really most interested in was not the observables per se, but the underlying pattern (structure) the observables eventually revealed” (p. 162).

It is precisely the underlying patterns in the analytic relationship to which this investigation of the double bind hypothesis in contemporary psychoanalytic thought is devoted.

Dr. Ringstrom is faculty at the Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles and at the California Institute for Clinical Social Work.

I wish to express my deepest appreciation to the following friends and colleagues for their encouraging comments and helpful criticisms of earlier drafts of this paper: Lew Aron, George Atwood, Howard Bacal, Tony Bass, Bernie Brandchaft, Jack Dender, Jim Fosshage, Irwin Hoffman, Lynn Jacobs, Joe Lichtenberg, John Lindon, Elizabeth Lokan-Egdahl, Naomi Malin, Donald Marcus, Steve Mitchell, Thomas Ogden, Loretta Polish, Owen Renik, Estelle and Mort Shane, Marcia Steinberg, Steven Stern, Bob Stolorow, Gary Sattler, Norm Tabachnick, and Judith Vida.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Postmodern Epistemology: The Problem of Validation and the Retreat from Therapeutics in Psychoanalysis

Michael Bader DMH

Michael Bader

2345 California St.
San Francisco, CA 94115
E-mail: drbader@aol.com

Michael Bader  

Social-constructivist and other postmodern currents within contemporary psychoanalysis put a great premium on an epistemological critique of positivism and the authority of the analyst. This focus on the essential ambiguity and constructed nature of experience implicitly tilts the analyst's interest away from a disciplined attempt to develop systems of validation that rely on observable patient responses as confirmatory data with which to judge the analyst's interventions and understandings. As a result, even while defending themselves against charges of relativism and solipsism, many postmodern writers still tend to idealize uncertainty and implicitly discourage the clinician from seeking greater accuracy and clarity about the patient. Epistemic doubt and an ethic that celebrates surprise, although useful as a corrective to tendencies toward rigidity and arrogance in technique, unnecessarily clouds the ubiquitous existence and possibility of accurate intersubjective understanding. This bias can be seen as historically linked to similar processes in academia in which progressive intellectuals abandoned a social change agenda in response to a growing political conservatism and cultural cynicism and, instead, became increasingly involved in “theory for its own sake.” Similarly, the epistemological position of the postmodernists sponsors a bias against therapeutic activism and inadvertently rationalizes a growing pessimism in our own clinical practice.

Gender Anxiety, Gender Melancholia, Gender Perversion

James Hansell PHD

James Hansell

3251 Bluett Road
Ann Arbor, MI 48105
email: Jhansell@umich.edu

James Hansell  

In this essay, I examine links among gender, sexual attraction, and sexual orientation rooted in the role of loss and mourning in the process of gender identity development. Building on the work of Fast (1984) and Butler (1995), I describe some of the psychological losses inherent in gender identity development and trace their effects on the structure of adult gender identity and sexual relationships. I propose that many important phenomena in the arena of gender and sexuality can be illuminated from this perspective, including a hidden perverse element in the structure of many adult heterosexual relationships. These speculations are offered in the context of current efforts to understand the psychodynamics of heterosexuality—a topic that has been neglected as a result of the long-standing tendency in the psychoanalytic literature to problematize (and pathologize) only “deviant” sexualities (Chodorow, 1992).

What's Disclosed in Self-Disclosures? Gender, Sexuality, and the Analyst's Subjectivity: Commentary on Paper by Samuel Gerson

Lynne Layton PHD and Katherine Bertone BA

Katherine Bertone

1101 Beacon Street, #6W
Brookline, MA 02146

1101 Beacon Street, #6W
Brookline, MA 02146

Katherine Bertone  

In the course of working on a thesis designed to gauge whether there were qualitative differences between the clinical work of self-professed analytic feminists working in the relational paradigm and the clinical work of those working in the relational paradigm who did not call themselves feminists, we came upon Samuel Gerson's (1996) “Neutrality, Resistance, and Self-Disclosure in an Intersubjective Psychoanalysis.” In this important contribution to a two-person psychology, Gerson argues that traditional views of neutrality seem in part to have been motivated by misogyny, a fear of becoming enmeshed in the seductions of female patients. Redefining neutrality as a relational event, Gerson proposes the existence of intersubjective resistances and a relational unconscious, the latter defined as a product of the “reciprocal and mutual influence of unconscious minds on one another” (p. 632).

Lynne Layton, Ph.D. is Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychology, Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School at the Beth Israel-Deaconess Medical Center; Visiting Faculty, Committee on Degrees in Women's Studies and Committee on Degrees in Social Studies, Harvard University; and Faculty, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis. She is the author of Who's That Girl? Who's That Boy? Clinical Practice Meets Postmodern Gender Theory.

Katherine Logan Bertone received a B.A. with honors from Harvard University in June 1997, completed a White House Internship at the Office for Women's Initiatives, and is currently associate producer of To the Contrary, a television series on women's issues.

Social Construction and Psychoanalytic Promise: Commentary on Paper by Michael J. Bader

Kenneth Gergen PHD

Kenneth Gergen

244 Myrtle Street
San Francisco, CA 94109

Kenneth Gergen  

In responding to Bader's challenging discussion of the postmodern retreat from psychoanalysis, it is first essential to position myself. Epistemological critiques of objectivity, truth, essentialism, universalism, and the narrative of progress can generally be traced to two primary, but quite different, logics. On the one hand, there is a family of what may be viewed as psychologically based critiques. Whether growing from the soil of Continental philosophy, psychoanalytic theory, constructivist psychology, Gadamerian hermeneutics, critical theory, or the Kuhnian account of scientific revolution, the general presumption is that mental process exerts a force on the individual's perception and/or conception of reality. Because there is no account of the real that is not influenced by mental process, there is no “god's eye view” enabling us to adjudicate competing accounts, thus no truth, objectivity, or progress that can transcend the individual standpoint.

In contrast to the psychological approach, a second domain of epistemological critique is essentially social in character. Issuing largely from dialogues in semiotics, poststructuralist literary theory, rhetorical theory, Wittgensteinian philosophy, social studies of science, and discourse analysis, the primary emphasis in this case is on the relation between language and its object. Because “whatever is” makes no necessary demands on our language (descriptions, explanations)

Kenneth J. Gergen is the Mustin Professor of Psychology, Swarthmore College, Swarthmore, PA. He is the Associate Editor of Theory and Psychology and The American Psychologist and is a cofounder of The Taos Institute. His written works include The Saturated Self (1991), Toward Transformation in Social Knowledge (1993), and Realities and Relationships (1994).

Sheldon Bach PHD

Sheldon Bach

365 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: docbach@erols.com

Sheldon Bach  

This article discusses binary, or dichotomous, thinking as it develops in normal classification, in pathological splitting, and in shifts of cultural awareness such as that from Classicism to Romanticism. It relates these to states of subjective and objective self-awareness as induced and regulated by the early parental environment. A number of clinical examples demonstrate how pathological splitting may arise and how the therapeutic process works to alter dichotomous thinking and raise it to higher levels of integration. It also suggests that a similar process of integration can take place over time in our historical understanding of cultural paradigm shifts.

Child Psychoanalysis, Adult Psychoanalysis, and Developmental Psychology: Introduction to Symposium on Child Analysis, Part II

Stephen Seligman DMH

Stephen Seligman

21 Vicksburg Street
San Francisco, CA 94114

Stephen Seligman  

The following collection of papers is the second and final part of a special symposium on child analysis that began in the previous issue of this journal. The papers in that issue suggested directions for reawakening the dormant child analytic tradition: from apparently divergent positions, they advanced a relationship-oriented clinical perspective that comprehends the pervasive and autonomous role of psychic reality without subordinating the actuality of social interactions to it. Although those papers were clinically focused, the current group of papers, by Jay B. Frankel, Peter Fonagy and Mary Target, and Susan Coates, extends these possibilities to the more conceptual and empirical exchanges between child analysis and the fields with which it is in greatest proximity—developmental psychology and adult analysis itself. These dialogues have often been awkward and uncertain, and child analysts have lagged behind adult analysts in incorporating developmental findings as well as in generating new theories of therapeutic action and technique. Although clinical thinking about adult analysis has been transformed in the last decades, many child analysts remain snared in an awkward compromise with loyalty to constricting clinical theories and have failed to explicate the clinical implications of their own flexible approach to children and families.

Stephen Seligman, D.M.H. is Clincial Professor of Psychiatry, Infant-Parent Program, University of California, San Francisco; Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; and Faculty, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Self in Conversation: On Narratives, Chronicles, and Scripts

Russell Meares MD

Russell Meares

Sydney, New South Wales 2145, Australia

Russell Meares  

In this paper, self is conceived as a particular form of mental activity, or process, the dynamic and moving form of which is manifest in the stream of consciousness (James, 1890) and in certain kinds of conversation. A developmental pathway is constructed for self seen in this way.

The development schema depends on the postulate of two fundamentally different forms of human language and conversation. One of the conversations, which has a nonlinear form, becomes a linguistic marker of “self.” The emergence of “self” is seen to depend on a form of memory, the autobiographical, which emerges at about four years of age. This memory system also allows the formation of narrative and of the storying that is the basis of culture.

Disruptions of self are conceived according to Jacksonian theory and the notion of trauma. They result in forms of consciousness that are underpinned by memory systems, which, relative to the “autobiographical,” are more primitive. The disruptions are manifest in clinical conversations of a less complex, more automatic kind than the narrative of self. They are referred to, in this article, as chronicles and scripts. Successful therapy involves their transformation into a more spontaneous and complex narrative form.

[[RunningHead]]

Flirting, Post-Oedipus, and Mutual Protectiveness in the Analytic Dyad: Commentary on Paper by Jody Messler Davies

Steven Cooper PHD

Steven Cooper

875 Massachusetts Avenue, #54
Cambridge, MA 02139
scoop13066@aol.com

Steven Cooper  

It's a sobering realization for any psychoanalyst who writes about her work that the patient and analyst whose work is being described know each other far more intimately than the reader to whom the analysis is being described. This puts in some context both the courage that fuels Jody Davies's continuing effort to explore the complexity of sexuality in psychoanalytic theory and practice and the considerable demands on us to consider her work.

There are many issues worth exploring in her article—the place of oedipal and preoedipal development in our general theory of sexuality and development (in particular, the place of a developmental model within American relational theory); the variety of levels of interpretation and metaphor in analytic work, including the interpersonal-intrapsychic and imaginary-symbolic dialectics; the ways in which trauma truncates play in general and sexual play in particular and their implications for analytic therapy; and differences among analysts in their tolerance and creativity in the expressive use of countertransference. The issue of disclosure, as that of any dominant metaphor, defensively reduces the complexity of so many of these issues into the either-or structure of “Should she say this or not?” All issues become equally magnetized by the pull of this intervention and its emotional arc.

Stephen H. Cooper, Ph.D., is Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Faculty and Supervising Analyst, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Associate Professor of Psychology, Beth Israel Hospital, Harvard Medical School.

[[RunningHead]]

Bigger Than Both of Us—Double Binds, Conflicting Interests, and the Inherent Paradoxes in Human Relating: Commentary on Paper by Ringstrom

Malcolm Slavin PHD and Daniel Kriegman PHD

Daniel Kriegman

112 Lakeview Avenue
Cambridge, MA 02138
email: mslavin@emerald.tufts.edu

Daniel Kriegman  

Clearly, it has been immensely helpful for Philip Ringstrom to make use of the double bind paradigm through the repeated, frustrating “damned if you do, damned if you don't” dilemmas in his inspiring, devoted work with Charles. Thinking in terms of the double bind seems to have enabled him to achieve a way of steering a course through those wrenching moments which, above all, repeatedly challenge the therapist's sense of hope. In the struggle to maintain both his and Charles's sense of hope—while respecting both their separate identities and needs—we believe we can sense the outlines of how the use of the double bind paradigm enabled Ringstrom to steer his own therapeutic course, a course that is informed by, yet does not fall readily into, the waiting categories of self-psychologically, relationally, or classically oriented technique. The double bind paradigm seems to have enabled Ringstrom to sustain the ongoing negotiation of a problematic relationship when theoretical loyalty failed to provide adequate guidance and, above all, to reach for a sense that he and Charles were struggling with something together, something that, perhaps in several ways, was bigger than both of them.

What are some implications of using the double bind model as the conceptual means to arrive at the sense of being part of a larger process? We approach this question by looking closely at the clinical material—the set of clinical experiences that Ringstrom describes in terms of the double bind.

responseSet.title: The Desire for Self-Creation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Desire for Self-Creation

Carlo Strenger PHD

Carlo Strenger

4 Feinstein, #11
Tel Aviv 69123
Israel
E-mail: strenger@freud.tau.ac.il

Carlo Strenger  

Some people feel that aspects of their past or limitations of their present prevent them from living a life they experience as worth living. They embark on the project of fully re-creating themselves. This article traces various manifestations of the desire for self-creation. An extended clinical example shows how this project led a patient to the brink of death, because it was more important for her to feel that she had authorship over her life than to be alive. The desire for self-creation has found cultural expression in the Cartesian program of cleaning the mind from any external influence. In less sublimated forms, this desire can be found in bodybuilding and certain forms of sexual fantasy. This article ends by discussing psychoanalytic and existentialist approaches to the protest against fatedness, which motivates the project of self-creation.

[[RunningHead]]

Dualism, Self-Reflexivity, and Intersubjectivity: Commentary on Paper by Sheldon Bach

John Auerbach PHD

John Auerbach

P.O. Box 4000
Mountain Home, TN 37684-4000
E-mail: auerbach.john_s@mtn-home.va.gov

John Auerbach  

There are two types of people, it has been said—those who divide the world into two categories and those who do not. Sheldon Bach, on superficial reading, would appear to fit into the former group. A more careful reading of his article, however, indicates that Bach is not content merely to propose a dichotomy between subjective and objective self-awareness. He also attempts to describe how normal human functioning comes to involve an integration of these two perspectives, subjective and objective, on the self and how pathology involves a failure of this integration. Although his dualistic premises hamper his account of these developments, Bach's (1985, 1994) analysis of self-reflexivity provides a crucial mediation between contemporary Freudian theory and modern intersubjective views (e.g., Cavell, 1993; Benjamin, 1995; Aron, 1996). In this commentary, I argue that the reflexive self-awareness discussed by Bach emerges only within the context of intersubjectivity.

It is always a pleasure to read Bach's writing, mainly because of his ability to link abstract theoretical notions to evocative, phenomenological descriptions of clinical material.

John S. Auerbach, Ph.D. is a Staff Psychologist and the Coordinator of the Post-Traumatic Stress Program at the James H. Quillen Veterans Affairs Medical Center. He is also a Clinical Associate Professor in the Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences of the James H. Quillen College of Medicine at East Tennessee State University and a Research Affiliate in the Department of Psychiatry of Yale University School of Medicine.

I thank Sidney Blatt, Robert Bornstein, and Thomas Schact for their helpful feedback on this commentary.

Thoughts on the Nature of Desires: The Ambiguous, The Transitional, and The Poetic: Reply to Commentaries

Jody Davies PHD

Jody Davies

441 West End Avenue, #2C
New York, NY 10024

Jody Davies  

It is both an honor and a challenge to reply in full measure to the sincerity and serious thoughtfulness that Drs. Cooper, Gabbard, and Hoffman have brought to their reading of my work. After reading each of their commentaries several times over, I am left with the conviction that so little has been written on the topic of erotic transference-countertransference that we are all here engaged in the collaborative definition of a new conversation. Our questions to one another define the substance of a discourse, and, when we differ in our points of view, we merely begin to define the boundaries of developing clinical and theoretical innovation. If find myself disagreeing with very little that has been said in the three commentaries, and I am therefore afforded the privilege of not having to respond point for point but of being allowed to wander, if you will, and permit myself to muse about some thoughts that became stimulated and elaborated around my reading of these insightful and provocative essays. Where I may leave certain specific disagreements and challenges unresponded to, I do so out of a conviction that the questions left to ponder serve us better than premature answers. It is simply, in my estimation, too early in this conversation to presume “to know.”

On Desire

responseSet.title: Reply to Commentary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentary

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

251 West 71st Street
New York, NY 10023

Stephen Mitchell  

I am grateful to Ruth Stein for grappling with my essay with such thoughtfulness and intensity. Of all the things I have ever written, this paper has provoked the most feedback, and not infrequently readers have said that it made them think about their own personal lives and relationships. The issues Stein has picked up on and the way she struggles with them point to the centrality of these problems in all of our lives.

Before we get to those issues, I want to say a brief word on what Stein argues is my misreading of Freud. I don't really care terribly much what Freud might have said (or meant or did not mean) at various points in his enormous oeuvre. Freud's genius was so complex; he wrote so much; he was willing to be wildly inconsistent; his basic models changed several times without systematic integration. All this adds up to a body of work that can be organized and systematized in many, many different ways. As Schafer (1992) argued, the “Return to Freud” banner that has been the rallying cry of French psychoanalysis (and that Stein draws on as authoritative) is polemical, because there is no single, veridical Freud to return to.

When that recommendation is made as a simple absolute rather than as a prod to keep up one's reading of Freud, it suggests wrongly that anyone could hope to read Freud in an absolutely selfless, value-free, culture-free, nondialogic way and thereby reach not only the right answer for the moment but the one and only true Freud. “Back to Freud!” is perhaps the greatest rhetorical ploy of all. The advisor is saying “Back to my Freud; repress the rest” [p. 152].

Dr. Mitchell, Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, is a Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute and is on the faculty of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

© 1998 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

Conflicting Interests and the Creation of a “Third Space”: Reply to Commentary

Malcolm Slavin PHD and Daniel Kriegman PHD

Daniel Kriegman

112 Lakeview Avenue
Cambridge, MA 02138
email: mslavin@emerald.tufts.edu

Daniel Kriegman  

On reading her stimulating discussion of our paper, we suspect that Benjamin hears ominous philosophical connotations in the notion of “conflicts of interest” as it has been used historically in discourses in which singular, “true” individual (or class) interests can be objectively known and rationally pursued. Space does not permit a fuller, theoretical clarification. Suffice it to say that, for us, the reason that self-interest is a vital, useful category of human interaction lies in the monumental, adaptive challenge posed by the paradoxical fact that we must all struggle (consciously and unconsciously) to look out for ourselves, yet must do so precisely: 1) without having any objective way of knowing what our true interests are; and, 2) without having any way of neatly separating our interests from the interests of others (some of whom we must “use” to construct our basic sense of self).

Clinically, Benjamin seems concerned that, in viewing the tensions in the therapeutic relationship as entailing a dimension of “conflicts of interest,” we run the danger of becoming overly literal—of concretely interpreting the patient's wishes and demands as valid needs, losing our sense of symbolic meanings, and abandoning our own vital commitment to the analytic space as a framework that ultimately transcends both our own interests and the patient's current subjective sense of her or his own interests. Benjamin implies that in so doing we risk getting lost in what is presumably “real” and thus sacrifice the potential to transform the collision of subjectivities, in patient and analyst, into a “thirdness”—an intersubjectivity that does not negate vital, individual, subjective differences.

© 1998 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

The Struggle for Recognition: Disruption and Reintegration in the Experience of Agency

Linda Pollock Psy.D. and Jonathan Slavin PHD

Jonathan Slavin

Medford, MA 02155

Jonathan Slavin  

In classical psychoanalytic models, treatment can be understood as enabling patients to repossess their own agency—to take responsibility for themselves as agents in relation to their own motives and impulses (“Where id was, there shall ego be”). How the question of agency is understood developmentally and as part of the treatment process in relational psychoanalytic perspectives has been less clearly articulated.

In this article, we suggest that the attainment of the ability to experience oneself as an agent emerges from complex interpersonal and intrapsychic processes in infancy and early childhood and represents a kind of “glue” that provides the foundation for a feeling of personal coherence. We also suggest that the development of a sense of agency is predicated on the negotiation of recognition and mutual impact with parents early in life. When those negotiations go awry, the child's capacity for agency can be disrupted. We suggest that many of the difficulties patients experience in their lives can be understood in this developmental context.

An earlier version of this article was presented at a symposium at the spring meeting of the Division of psychoanalysis (39), Denver, CO, February, 1997. Thanks to Jody Davies, Ph.D., Irene Fast, Ph.D., Patricia Hertz, LICSW, Miki Rahmani, M.A., Natalie Shavit, M.A., Jessica Slavin, and Hanoch Yerushalmi, Ph.D., for their careful reading and comment. Some of the central ideas elaborated here were developed in a chapter by Jonathan H. Slavin, Ph.D. (1997).

Linda Pollock, Psy.D. is a member of the faculty, Boston Institute for Psychotherapy; past president, Massachusetts Association for Psychoanalytic Psychology; and Consulting Psychologist, Counseling Center, Tufts University, Medford, MA.

Jonathan H. Slavin, Ph.D. is Director, Counseling Center, Tufts University, Medford, MA; past-president, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; past president, Division of Psychoanalysis (39), American Psychological Association; Scholar in Residence, Counseling Service, The Hebrew University, Jerusalem, Israel; and Honorary Chair, Psychoanalytic Division, Wuhan Branch, China Medical Association.

From this perspective, the crux of psychoanalytic treatment often hinges on the renegotiation of recognition and mutual impact, which facilitates the restoration of the patient's sense of personal agency not only in relation to internal impulses but also in the relational world. We elaborate these issues with a clinical example and explore the challenge to analysts in negotiating both their own and their patients' agency in a complex relational context.

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentary

Susan Sands PHD

Susan Sands

1664 Solano Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94707

Susan Sands  

As I have come to expect of their work, Stolorow, Orange and Atwood's reply to my article is logically and epistemologically lucid, rigorous, and clever. However, much of their reply addresses assertions I did not make and beliefs I do not hold. It creates a cartoon version of projective identification, then attempts to punch holes in that.

First of all, many of their criticisms seem to be aimed at the preposterous notion that the analyst's visceral states can always be directly attributed to the patient's unconscious intentionality. I do not believe this. I believe that some of the analyst's experiences are strongly influenced by the patient—at the same time that they are always shaped by the analyst's unique psychology. I believe that we must be very tentative and humble in exploring who did what to whom. The patient's unconscious intentionality is of course but one part of a “dynamic system of intercoordinated elements” determining any interaction. And, yes, of course the analyst's projective identifications affect the patient's experience as well. In short, I think Stolorow et al. and I agree theoretically on the intersubjective, nonlinear, and dynamic nature of all interaction; where we disagree is in the arena of how such intersubjective influence comes about.

Indeed, Stolorow et al. appear to oscillate between two contradictory positions—on the one hand, their theoretical stance of intersubjectivity, including “reciprocal mutual influence,” and, on the other hand, their more emotional stand that analysts should take sole responsibility for their own troublesome affect states. They seem to be arguing for intersubjectivity and “isolated mind” at the same time. They assert throughout their work that patients and analysts influence
